content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
11102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 27, 2017

Gary W. Schons
Counsel for WRCOG
Best Best & Krieger, LLP
655 West Broadway, 15th Floor
San Diego, Ca 92101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-195

Dear Mr. Schons:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Western Riverside Council of Governments ("WRCOG") regarding Government Code Section 1090.¹ We are only providing advice regarding Section 1090, and we offer no opinion on the applicability of other conflict of interest laws. We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to it to the Attorney General's Office and the Riverside County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does Section 1090 prohibit WRCOG from entering into a contract with EES Consulting for Community Choice Aggregation ("CCA") agency operations given that EES Consulting was a subcontractor on a prior WRCOG contract to conduct a feasibility study of potential organizational scenarios for a CCA or multiple CCAs within the region?

CONCLUSION

No. Section 1090 does not prohibit WRCOG from entering into the contract with EES Consulting because EES Consulting is not subject to Section 1090 with respect to the CCA agency operations contract.

¹ All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-195
Page No. 2

FACTS

Authorized pursuant to Public Utilities Code Section 366.2, CCA enables city and county
governments to pool the electricity demand within their jurisdictions to directly procure or generate
electrical power supplies on behalf of the residents and businesses in their communities. While
electricity supply is purchased by the CCA, the incumbent utility remains responsible for
maintaining the electricity grid and for providing customer service.

On May 2, 2016, WRCOG, in coordination with the Coachella Valley Association of
Governments ("CVAG") and the San Bernandino Associated Governments ("SANBAG"),² entered
into a contract with Bevilacqua-Knight, Inc. ("BK") for a feasibility study of potential
organizational scenarios for a CCA, referred to as Inland Choice Power ("ICP"), or multiple CCAs
within the region. You state that EES Consulting was a subcontractor for BK.

On December 8, 2016, EES Consulting submitted the "Inland Choice Power Community
Choice Aggregation Business Plan" (the "ICP Business Plan") to WRCOG, CVAG, and SANBAG
as its work product under the feasibility study contract. The ICP Business Plan considered two
primary organizational scenarios for the potential CCA(s): one in which one organization or agency
would operate a CCA for all three agencies (the "ICP Scenario"), and another in which three
separate CCAs would be formed. With respect to the ICP Business Plan's conclusion and
recommendation, its "Executive Summary" provides as follows:

"This plan concludes that the formation of ICP in the service areas of CVAG,
SANBAG, and WRCOG is financially prudent and will yield considerable
benefits for ICP's residents and businesses. ... A joint CCA with common
back office functions and local branding as opposed to three separate CCAs is
the most economical operational option and is also recommended."

Acting on the recommendations of its CCA Ad Hoc Committee and its Administration &
Finance Committee, however, WRCOG has since rejected the ICP Business Plan's recommendation
of the ICP Scenario, and has determined it will instead form a CCA exclusively within its own
service area.

On March 3, 2017, WRCOG, in cooperation with CVAG and SANBAG, issued a request
for proposals for "Community Choice Aggregation Agency Operations" (the "CCA Operations
RFP"). Because the geographical jurisdiction of the potential CCA(s) had yet to be determined, the
CCA Operations RFP was written in a manner whereby respondents were asked to provide bids on
specific tasks related to CCA operations for each of the following potential organizational scenarios:

• One CCA created for both Riverside and San Bernandino Counties.

• Two CCAs created – one for Riverside County and one for San Bernandino County.

_______________
² As of January 2017, the San Bernandino Associated Governments (SANBAG) is known as the San
Bernandino Council of Governments. However, for consistency and clarity, we will utilize its prior acronym.

File No. A-17-195
Page No. 3

• Three CCAs created – one for each geographical area corresponding to the respective
service areas of CVAG, SANBAG, and WRCOG.

WRCOG received nine proposals in response to the CCA Operations RFP, including a
proposal from EES Consulting. WRCOG staff reviewed those proposals, and then recommended
that the WRCOG Executive Committee select The Energy Authority and EES Consulting to
provide services under the potential contract for CCA agency operations.

You have asked whether Section 1090 prohibits WRCOG from entering into that potential
contract for CCA agency operations with EES Consulting given that EES Consulting submitted the
ICP Business Plan as a subcontractor on the WRCOG feasibility study contract.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090 provides, in pertinent part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county,
district, judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any
contract made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are
members."

Interpreting the term "employees" as used in Section 1090, the California Supreme Court
recently affirmed the long-standing rule from case law that independent contractors are not
categorically excluded from Section 1090: "Liability under the statute can extend to independent
contractors who have duties to engage in or advise on public contracting." (People v. Superior
Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230, 233.) For example, a "corporate consultant" serving as an
independent contractor for a state or local government agency that "has a hand in designing and
developing the plans and specifications for the project" is subject to Section 1090. (Davis v. Fresno
Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300-301.)

However, in Sahlolbei, the Supreme Court stated that "section 1090 liability extends only to
independent contractors who can be said to have been entrusted with 'transact[ing] on behalf of the
Government.' (Stgall, supra, 58 Cal.2d at p. 570). (See Hub City, supra, 186 Cal.App.4th at p.
1125 ['An individual's status as an official under [section 1090] turns on the extent to which the
person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a capacity that demands the
public trust.']." (Sahlolbei, supra, at p. 240.)

The ICP Business Plan provided by EES Consulting pursuant to WRCOG's feasibility study
contract with BK was advisory in nature; it analyzed multiple potential organizational structures for
a CCA or multiple CCAs within the region, reached conclusions and made recommendations, but
did not purport to make any governmental decisions on behalf of WRCOG, CVAG, or SANBAG.
Furthermore, the facts presented provide no indication of any rubber-stamping of EES Consulting's
conclusions or recommendations set forth in the ICP Business Plan, as WRCOG rejected its
recommendations. Therefore, EES Consulting's influence on WRCOG was limited, and EES
Consulting cannot be said to have "transacted on behalf of" WRCOG with respect to the feasibility
study contract. Accordingly, EES Consulting is not subject to Section 1090 with respect to the CCA
agency operations contract based on the facts presented.

File No. A-17-195
Page No. 4

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

[signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

sections.conclusion:
No. Section I 090 does not prohibit WRCOG from entering into the contract with EES 
Consulting because EES Consulting is not subject to Section I 090 with respect to the CCA agency 
operations contract.

Section 1090 provides, in pertinent part, that "(m)embers of the Legislature, state, county,

developing the plans and specifications for the project" is subject to Section 1090. (Davis v. Fresno

However, in Sahlolbei, the Supreme Court stated that "section I 090 liability extends only to 
independent contractors who can be said to have been entrusted with 'transact[ing] on behalf of the

1125 (' An individual's status as an official under [section 1090] turns on the extent to whi,ch the 
person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a capacity that demands the

The ICP Business Plan provided by EES Consulting pursuant to WRCOG's feasibility study

Community Choice Aggregation ("CCA") agency operations given that EES Consulting was a

scenarios for a CCA or multiple CCAs within the region?
